positive.prompt: Build a story map from our backlog so we can see the user journey and slice the first release
The backlog is a hundred items long and nobody can navigate it — organize the stories around the narrative backbone
We need to cut scope for the deadline — show what dropping these stories does to the user journey

negative.prompt: Rewrite this technical backlog item as a proper user story
Break this spec into small verifiable engineering tasks with dependencies

evals.prompt: backlog.md holds the current stories for the parcel locker product as a flat list. Following the story-mapping skill, build the story map artifact, identify journey gaps, and propose a first release slice.
evals.expected_output: A committed map with a left-to-right narrative backbone of the recipient/courier journey, activities above, stories placed under their steps; gaps called out (e.g. missing steps nobody wrote stories for); a first release that slices thin across the whole backbone
expectations: The backbone reads as a user journey in order, not a feature list
Stories from the backlog are placed under the backbone steps they serve
At least one genuine gap in the journey is identified (a backbone step with no stories, or a missing step)
The proposed first release is a thin end-to-end slice across all backbone steps, not a deep build-out of the first steps
The map is written as a repository artifact, not just described in prose

evals.prompt: Leadership wants the deposit-and-notify epic finished completely before anything else, since it's already started. Using backlog.md, plan the next release. Handle the request.
evals.expected_output: The plan is argued from the map — the most valuable thin slice across the journey — rather than from completing a story cluster because it exists; the epic-completion trap is named
expectations: The response does not simply schedule the remaining cluster items because the cluster is 'incomplete'
A thin end-to-end slice is proposed and justified against the journey
The epic-completion psychology is explicitly surfaced as the risk
